step.title: Step 30
step.actionDisplay: SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE -> SPACE

Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The goal is to fill the black bar on the right edge to full height (63/64). RIGHT, DOWN, and SPACE each increment the bar by 1 when they move something. The bar is currently at 27, so I need 36 more increments. Since I'm already over budget, I'll just batch SPACE actions to finish as fast as possible.
- Plan: Batch 36 SPACE actions to fill the bar completely.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 76 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 2.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The bar grew from 102 to 103 pixels after 36 SPACE actions - only 1 pixel growth! But looking at the transition history, each SPACE action DOES increment the bar by 1. The issue is that when I batch 36 SPACE actions, only the LAST one's effect is visible in the transition (because transitions only record the final before/after).
Wait, looking more carefully at the data:
- T12: RIGHT, bar 103 -> 114 (diff=11) - RIGHT jumps the bar by 11!
- T13-T14: RIGHT, +1 each
- T17-T75: SPACE/RIGHT/DOWN, +1 each
So RIGHT gives a big jump (+11) while SPACE and DOWN only give +1. The bar is currently at 166 pixels. I need to figure out what the target is. Let me check what the full bar height would be.
Actually wait - the bar is on the right edge. Let me think about what "full height" means. The board is 64x64. If the bar needs to go from top to bottom, that's 64 pixels. But the bar has 166 pixels now, which is way more than 64. 
Let me reconsider. The bar might be filling from top to bottom, and 166 pixels means it's already quite full. Or maybe the goal isn't just about the bar height. Let me look at the actual board state more carefully.
Actually, looking at the boundary `[[0, 63], [62, 63], [51, 63], [50, 62], [50, 60], [45, 60], [44, 59], [44, 54], [42, 54], [42, 62], [41, 63]]` - this describes a shape on the right edge. The bar goes from row 0 to row 62 at column 63, with some structure.
Let me think differently. Maybe the goal is to get some object to a certain position, not just fill a bar. Let me look at what objects are on the board and what RIGHT does.
Actually, RIGHT gave +11 to the bar at T12. That's a huge jump. Maybe RIGHT moves an object that then "fills in" a big chunk of the bar. Let me try RIGHT again to see if it still gives a big jump.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current bar state and test RIGHT for big jump
nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
bar_nodes = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'B' and n['pixels'] > 50]
for n in bar_nodes:
    print(f"Bar: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check what objects are on the board
print(f"\nAll nodes:")
for n in nodes:
    print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, hash={n['hash']}, boundary={n['boundary'][:4]}...")

# Try RIGHT to see if it gives a big jump
result = action(['RIGHT'])
after_nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
after_bars = [n for n in after_nodes if n['color'] == 'B' and n['pixels'] > 50]
for n in after_bars:
    print(f"\nAfter RIGHT - Bar: pixels={n['pixels']}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
